skill.name: postgresql
skill.description: Design PostgreSQL schemas with practical guidance for data types, constraints, indexes, partitioning, RLS, and safe migration planning.
skill.summary: Guide PostgreSQL schema design, indexing, constraints, partitioning, and safe schema evolution.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, sensitive data, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found these are false positives caused by Markdown inline SQL examples, PostgreSQL terminology, and database design guidance rather than executable code. The skill is safe to publish with normal caution because it advises schema changes that should be tested before production use.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings are Markdown inline SQL and PostgreSQL examples, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation. Lines such as EXPLAIN guidance, identity column syntax, and CREATE TABLE examples are documentation content with no executable wrapper.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown guide and contains no Ruby code, shell scripts, subprocess calls, or command execution APIs. The flagged backticks are Markdown code formatting around SQL terms and examples.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Windows SAM Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The critical sensitive-data finding at the Row-Level Security section is PostgreSQL policy syntax. It does not reference the Windows SAM database, credential stores, or local system files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 90 contains ALTER TABLE and CREATE POLICY examples for PostgreSQL RLS. There is no evidence of Windows registry paths, SAM files, credential dumping, or data access outside PostgreSQL schema design.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography And Reconnaissance Detections Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings are triggered by database vocabulary such as HASH partitioning, pgcrypto, crypt(), UUID generation, and DDL guidance. These are legitimate PostgreSQL design topics and do not show malware intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content discusses PostgreSQL partitioning, extensions, and schema evolution. It does not include password extraction, weak algorithm implementation, host reconnaissance commands, or hidden execution paths.

low_findings.title: Production DDL Guidance Requires Operator Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples and recommendations for schema changes, partitioning, indexes, and constraints. These are legitimate, but users should review generated DDL before applying it to production databases.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly discusses production safety, migrations, and DDL rollback planning. This is not malicious, but direct application of generated database changes can create availability or data integrity risk.

content.value_statement: PostgreSQL schema choices affect performance, correctness, and migration safety. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PostgreSQL-specific tables, indexes, constraints, partitioning, and safe evolution.
actual_capabilities: Guides PostgreSQL table design using data types, primary keys, constraints, and defaults.
Explains index choices for B-tree, GIN, GiST, BRIN, partial, expression, and covering indexes.
Covers partitioning patterns for time-series, regional, and hash-distributed tables.
Provides guidance for JSONB, arrays, ranges, full-text search, and extension-backed features.
Highlights safe schema evolution practices for migrations, concurrent indexes, and rollback planning.
Includes example table patterns for users, orders, and JSONB profile data.
limitations: It focuses on PostgreSQL and is not a database-agnostic modeling guide.
It does not connect to a database or validate live query plans by itself.
It does not replace review by a database administrator for production migrations.
It gives schema design guidance, not full application architecture or ORM integration.

use_cases.title: Plan A New Product Schema
use_cases.description: Design tables, keys, constraints, and indexes for a new PostgreSQL-backed product before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review A Migration Proposal
use_cases.description: Check planned DDL for safe defaults, constraint behavior, index impact, and rollback considerations.

use_cases.title: Model High-Volume Data
use_cases.description: Choose partitioning, BRIN indexes, insert-friendly keys, and retention patterns for large event or time-series tables.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PostgreSQL skill to design tables for [domain]. Include primary keys, foreign keys, required fields, indexes, and short reasons for each choice.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PostgreSQL skill to review this table design for correctness, constraints, data types, and indexes. Explain the top risks and suggest safer alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Scale And Partitioning
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PostgreSQL skill to design a schema for [workload] with [row count], [QPS], and [retention]. Recommend indexes, partitioning, and migration steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Production Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PostgreSQL skill to audit this migration plan. Identify locking risk, rewrite risk, rollback options, concurrent index needs, and production validation steps.

output_examples.input: Design user and order tables for a commerce app.
output: A normalized table plan with users and orders separated by clear foreign keys.
Recommended constraints for required fields, status values, and positive totals.
Indexes for user lookups, order ownership, and creation-time filters.

output_examples.input: Review a table that stores optional profile attributes.
output: A recommendation to keep core relational fields as columns and use JSONB only for variable attributes.
Index guidance for containment queries and extracted scalar fields.
Validation suggestions to keep JSONB values shaped consistently.

output_examples.input: Plan a migration for a large events table.
output: A phased migration plan that separates table creation, backfill, index creation, and validation.
Partitioning guidance based on retention and query filters.
Warnings about locking, volatile defaults, and production rollback planning.

best_practices: Provide expected access patterns, row counts, retention needs, and write rates before asking for schema design.
Ask for rationale with each data type, constraint, index, and partitioning recommendation.
Validate generated DDL in staging with representative data before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill for non-PostgreSQL databases without adapting the recommendations.
Do not create indexes for every possible query without measuring write and storage cost.
Do not apply destructive DDL to production without backups, rollback steps, and maintenance planning.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete PostgreSQL schemas?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide table definitions, constraints, indexes, and migration planning from your requirements.

faq.question: Can it tune slow queries?
faq.answer: It can suggest schema and index changes, but it is not a full query tuning workflow without EXPLAIN output.

faq.question: Does it support JSONB design?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers JSONB indexing, generated columns, containment queries, and when to keep data relational.

faq.question: Can it help with row-level security?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes PostgreSQL RLS concepts and policy planning, but policies need application-specific review.

faq.question: Is it safe for production migrations?
faq.answer: It includes safety guidance, but every migration should be tested in staging and reviewed before production.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
